description:
## Problem Statement

Beadsmith users cannot currently share or reopen a direct reference to an Issue. An Issue ID alone is insufficient because the same ID may exist in more than one Workspace. Moving among Issues, Issue List Views, and status-specific views also loses the user's browsing context: there is no browser-like Back or Forward navigation, and Issue References in Issue Detail are not navigable.

This makes investigation and triage slower. Users must manually select the correct Workspace, locate the right Issue List View, clear filters that hide an Issue, and search for its ID. They also cannot easily return through the Issues and views they recently inspected.

## Solution

Add deep links that identify an Issue Location using the `beadsmithy` custom URI scheme, the target Workspace path, and the Issue ID. Opening a link will resolve its path through the normal Workspace validation flow, ask before changing Workspaces, and open the target Issue in Issue Detail. Beadsmith will reuse and focus the existing application window.

Add browser-style session navigation backed by Wouter and the browser History API. Issue selection and Issue List View changes create Issue Navigation Entries; search edits update the current entry. Persistent Back and Forward controls and platform keyboard shortcuts traverse entries, including entries in other Workspaces. Issue rows and Issue References use real anchor semantics. Users can copy a canonical deep link from Issue Detail.

## User Stories

1. As a Beadsmith user, I want to open a link to a specific Issue, so that I can reach it without manually finding its Workspace and list row.
2. As a Beadsmith user, I want a deep link to identify both the Workspace and Issue ID, so that duplicate Issue IDs in different Workspaces are unambiguous.
3. As a Beadsmith user, I want deep links to use the `beadsmithy` scheme, so that my operating system can open them directly in the desktop application.
4. As a macOS user, I want packaged Beadsmith links to open the application, so that deep links work through normal macOS application integration.
5. As a Linux user, I want installed Beadsmith links to open the application, so that deep links work through normal desktop integration.
6. As a user with Beadsmith already running, I want a deep link to reuse and focus the existing window, so that opening a link does not create duplicate application instances.
7. As a user opening Beadsmith from a deep link, I want remembered Workspace restoration to settle before the link is resolved, so that I do not receive an unnecessary add-or-switch prompt.
8. As a user opening an Issue in the Current Workspace, I want it to open without a Workspace confirmation, so that ordinary links are immediate.
9. As a user opening an Issue in another Workspace, I want Beadsmith to ask before switching, so that an external link cannot silently replace my current context.
10. As a user opening an Issue in an unknown Workspace, I want the confirmation to explain that the Workspace will be added and opened, so that catalog changes are explicit.
11. As a user with no Current Workspace, I want a link to ask whether its Workspace should be added and opened, so that first-use behavior remains explicit.
12. As a user who accepts a cross-Workspace link, I want Beadsmith to use the normal Workspace-selection flow, so that validation, loading, and catalog persistence remain consistent.
13. As a user opening a link containing a Workspace subdirectory or symlink, I want Beadsmith to resolve the canonical Git root, so that one Workspace does not appear under duplicate identities.
14. As a user who declines a cross-Workspace link, I want my Current Workspace, Issue selection, and Workspace Catalog left unchanged, so that declining has no side effects.
15. As a user opening a malformed or unsupported link, I want a dismissible global error banner, so that failure is visible without disrupting my current context.
16. As a user who later opens a valid deep link, I want the prior link error banner cleared, so that stale errors do not remain after recovery.
17. As a user receiving another link while a confirmation is open, I want the prompt to update to the latest valid link, so that I do not face a queue of stale prompts.
18. As a user whose target Workspace cannot load, I want my current Workspace and Issue Explorer snapshot preserved with a global error banner, so that failed navigation is atomic.
19. As a user opening a deleted or unknown Issue ID in a valid Workspace, I want an “Issue not found” state in Issue Detail, so that I understand which part of navigation failed.
20. As a user viewing an Issue that disappears during refresh, I want its route to remain and Issue Detail to show “Issue not found,” so that refresh does not silently redirect me.
21. As a user opening an Issue hidden by the current Issue List View or search, I want Beadsmith to switch to All Issues and clear search as needed, so that the target is visible in the Issue List.
22. As a user deep-linking while Settings is open, I want Settings closed and the Issue opened, so that the link takes me to its destination.
23. As a user deep-linking while Settings is open, I want Back to return to the underlying Issue Explorer state rather than Settings, so that transient Settings does not pollute navigation history.
24. As a user navigating among Issues, I want each deliberate selection of a different Issue to create one history entry, so that I can retrace my investigation.
25. As a user selecting the already-current Issue, I want no duplicate history entry, so that Back does not repeat the same destination.
26. As a user changing an Issue List View or status-specific view, I want that change recorded in history, so that Back restores the previous browsing lens.
27. As a user changing to a view that excludes my selected Issue, I want the new entry to have no selection, so that the view and Issue Detail remain semantically coherent.
28. As a user editing Issue Search, I want the current history entry updated rather than a new entry added for each keystroke, so that history remains useful.
29. As a user editing Issue Search, I want my selected Issue to remain in Issue Detail even if its row becomes filtered out, so that search does not discard what I am reading.
30. As a user navigating Back or Forward, I want the destination Workspace, Issue List View, search query, and optional selected Issue restored together, so that the complete browsing context returns.
31. As a user navigating Back or Forward across Workspaces, I want the recorded Workspace restored without another confirmation, so that explicit history traversal is direct.
32. As a user whose cross-Workspace history destination fails to load, I want the current snapshot and route preserved with an error banner, so that history failure cannot leave mismatched UI.
33. As a user, I want persistent Back and Forward buttons, so that navigation is discoverable.
34. As a user, I want unavailable Back and Forward directions visibly disabled, so that navigation boundaries are clear.
35. As a user, I want navigation tooltips to describe the destination when available, so that I can anticipate where a control will take me.
36. As a macOS user, I want Command–Left Bracket and Command–Right Bracket shortcuts, so that navigation matches familiar browser behavior.
37. As a Linux user, I want Alt–Left and Alt–Right shortcuts, so that navigation matches familiar browser behavior.
38. As a user typing in a text field, I want ordinary editing interactions preserved, so that navigation shortcuts do not unexpectedly consume input behavior.
39. As a keyboard or screen-reader user opening a deep link or traversing history, I want focus moved to the Issue Detail heading or landmark, so that the destination is announced and oriented clearly.
40. As a user navigating to an Issue, I want Issue Detail to start at the top in version one, so that restored stale scroll positions do not disorient me.
41. As a user, I want the window title to identify the resolved Issue, Issue-not-found state, Settings, or general Issue List, so that application context is visible outside the content pane.
42. As a user, I want Issue List rows to be real links, so that keyboard, modifier-click, and context-menu semantics are consistent with navigation.
43. As a user, I want Parent, dependency, and Child Issue references in Issue Detail to be real links, so that related work is directly navigable.
44. As a user following an Issue Reference, I want the referenced Issue opened in the Current Workspace with one history entry, so that relationship exploration works like other Issue navigation.
45. As a user following an Issue Reference hidden by view or search, I want it revealed through All Issues with search cleared as needed, so that the linked Issue can be located in the list.
46. As a user following a missing Issue Reference, I want the same Issue-not-found detail state, so that all Issue navigation fails consistently.
47. As a user viewing a resolved Issue, I want a Copy deep link button in the top-right of Issue Detail, so that I can share or save its Issue Location.
48. As a user who copies a deep link, I want the button to show a checkmark and a “Copied!” tooltip temporarily, so that I know the operation succeeded without global noise.
49. As a user viewing an Issue-not-found state, I do not want a Copy deep link action, so that Beadsmith does not encourage sharing unresolved destinations.
50. As a user restarting Beadsmith, I want prior browsing history discarded, so that stale session context is not unexpectedly restored.
51. As a user launching Beadsmith through a deep link, I want the link to replace the meaningless initial empty history entry, so that Back does not lead to startup scaffolding.
52. As a user opening a deep link while Beadsmith is running, I want it to create a history entry, so that Back returns to what I was viewing.
53. As a user opening Settings and then closing it, I want my exact Workspace, Issue List View, search, and Issue selection restored, so that Settings does not disturb Issue navigation.
54. As a user using Back while Settings is open, I want navigation to traverse Issue Navigation Entries rather than preserve Settings as a history destination, so that Settings remains transient.

## Implementation Decisions

- The canonical external format is `beadsmithy:///absolute/workspace/path/issue/<issue-id>`. Workspace and Issue path segments are percent-encoded and decoded by a dedicated URI contract.
- An Issue Location consists of a Workspace and Issue ID. An Issue ID alone is not globally unique.
- Received Workspace paths enter the existing Workspace-selection validation transaction. The canonical Git root returned by that transaction is the identity used for Current Workspace comparison and catalog persistence.
- macOS and Linux are required version-one platforms. Windows support may be included only when the same Tauri configuration makes it effectively trivial; Windows work must not delay macOS or Linux.
- Tauri’s official Deep Link plugin will own installed-scheme registration and URL delivery. Startup links are read through the startup API; running links are received through its event API.
- Tauri’s official Single Instance integration will make an existing process receive and focus for Linux deep links. Plugin registration order must follow Tauri’s documented requirement. The corresponding Windows behavior may come along only if configuration is trivial.
- If one delivery contains multiple URLs, Beadsmith uses the last URL. No generalized link queue is introduced.
- A newer valid link supersedes any link awaiting Workspace confirmation.
- Startup Workspace restoration completes before a startup deep link is resolved. The startup deep link replaces the initial renderer entry; a running deep link pushes an entry.
- Cross-Workspace deep links require confirmation. Declining discards the request without changing the Workspace Catalog, Current Workspace, route, or Issue selection.
- Accepting a deep link delegates to the normal atomic Workspace switch. An unknown valid Workspace is added through that transaction. Workspace identity and Issue Explorer data remain atomically paired.
- Back and Forward across Workspaces are explicit user navigation and do not request confirmation. A failed historical Workspace switch preserves the current route and visible snapshot and raises the global error banner.
- Invalid or unsupported links never switch Workspaces or mutate the Workspace Catalog. Their global banner remains until dismissed or until a later deep link succeeds.
- Wouter’s full React navigation surface provides the renderer Router, Route matching, Link anchors, location navigation, and search-parameter synchronization.
- Wouter owns URL observation and renderer navigation primitives only. Beadsmith owns deep-link parsing, Workspace confirmation and switching, Issue resolution, failure policy, and Issue Navigation Entry semantics.
- Internal Issue routes use `/issues/<issue-id>` and `/issues` when no Issue is selected. `view` and `search` query parameters encode Issue List View and Issue Search; default values may be omitted.
- Settings uses `/settings` but is entered and left with replacement semantics. The exact underlying Issue Explorer destination is retained for restoration. A deep link closes Settings, restores that destination, and then pushes the deep-linked destination.
- Workspace paths do not appear in internal renderer URLs. Each Issue Navigation Entry carries its Workspace path in serializable browser history state.
- Issue Navigation Entries carry only Workspace path, optional Issue ID, Issue List View, search query, and a Beadsmith navigation index. They do not contain Issue data.
- The navigation index is used solely to determine custom Back and Forward boundaries. Issue data is always re-resolved from the current Beadwork-backed Issue List.
- Selecting a different Issue pushes one entry. Selecting the current Issue is a no-op.
- Changing an Issue List View, including status-specific views, pushes one entry. If the selected Issue is excluded by the destination view, the new entry has no selected Issue.
- Editing search replaces the current entry and preserves the selected Issue even when its row is filtered out.
- Deep links and navigable Issue References reveal hidden targets by selecting All Issues and clearing search when needed. The prior context remains recoverable through Back.
- A routed Issue that cannot be resolved renders Issue-not-found in Issue Detail and remains routed. A later data refresh does not silently clear that route.
- Issue List rows and every rendered Issue Reference—Parent, Blocked by, Blocking, Child Issues, and future reference presentations—use Wouter Link semantics.
- Persistent Back and Forward controls expose disabled states and destination tooltips. Shortcuts are Command–Left Bracket/Command–Right Bracket on macOS and Alt–Left/Alt–Right on Linux and Windows. Text-input handling must not be disrupted.
- Navigation through deep links and Back/Forward moves focus to a focusable Issue Detail heading or landmark after resolution. Issue Detail scroll restoration is not implemented in version one; navigation starts at the top.
- Window titles are `<issue-id> — <title> · Beadsmithy` for a resolved Issue, `Issue not found · Beadsmithy` for an unresolved route, `Settings · Beadsmithy` for Settings, and `Beadsmithy` for the Issue List.
- A Copy deep link icon button appears in the top-right of resolved Issue Detail. Success temporarily changes the icon to a checkmark and shows a “Copied!” tooltip. It produces no global feedback and is absent from Issue-not-found.
- Navigation history is session-only and is not added to application persistence.

## Testing Decisions

- Good tests assert behavior visible at a supported boundary: delivered links, Current Workspace, rendered route and Issue Detail, history traversal, focus, title, confirmation, banners, and catalog effects. Tests must not assert Wouter internals, React state layout, plugin implementation details, or private helper call order.
- The highest seam is a real built desktop acceptance suite. It should prove installed/custom-scheme delivery on macOS and Linux where the CI or local platform permits, startup handling, delivery to an existing instance, focus/reuse behavior, and a representative cross-Workspace flow against disposable Beadwork Workspaces and an isolated Workspace Catalog.
- The desktop suite should extend the existing WebDriver approach that launches the real built binary and drives typed Workspace behavior through the assembled Rust, TauRPC, and renderer stack. Scenario-owned stores and fixture Workspaces must preserve the existing isolation guarantees.
- Renderer integration tests at the application/navigation coordinator boundary should exercise same-Workspace links, cross-Workspace accept/decline, unknown Workspaces, latest-link-wins, failed switches, Settings restoration, startup replacement, running-link push, Back/Forward across Workspaces, search replacement, view pushes, missing Issues, global banners, focus movement, and window titles.
- The renderer integration seam should mock only external Tauri delivery and the existing typed RPC boundary. It should inspect accessible rendered behavior and browser history outcomes rather than component internals.
- Pure contract tests should exhaustively cover external URI generation and parsing: Unix roots, spaces and reserved characters, encoded path segments, `/issue/` within Workspace directory names, malformed percent encoding, missing or extra suffix components, unsupported schemes, empty Issue IDs, and Windows drive syntax if Windows support is included.
- Pure navigation contract tests should cover internal route parsing/serialization, default query omission, unknown Issue List View fallback, history-state validation, monotonic navigation indexes, duplicate-current navigation, and replacement versus push decisions.
- Existing Workspace transition-gate tests remain the prior art and authority for atomic Workspace identity/snapshot publication and stale generation rejection. Deep-link navigation must use this seam rather than duplicate its race rules.
- Existing Issue Explorer tests are prior art for selection, Issue List View, search, Issue Detail, Issue References, and missing/filtered data behavior. They should be updated from button expectations to accessible link expectations where product behavior changes.
- Existing application navigation and Settings tests are prior art for sidebar destinations and preserving Issue Explorer state while Settings is visible.
- Plugin configuration receives build/package verification in addition to unit tests because scheme registration cannot be proven by jsdom. Linux development may use the official debug/runtime registration mechanism, while release behavior is validated from an installed artifact.
- Verification gates include Rust unit tests, renderer tests, TypeScript build, Ultracite checks, Tauri debug build, and available macOS/Linux desktop acceptance scenarios.

## Out of Scope

- Persisting Issue Navigation Entries or scroll positions across application restarts.
- Restoring Issue Detail scroll position during Back or Forward navigation.
- HTTPS universal links or app links requiring domain ownership and association files.
- Portable cross-machine Workspace identity. Deep links remain local-machine-specific and expose an absolute filesystem path.
- Multiple windows or one tab per deep link.
- A generalized queue or batch UI for multiple URLs delivered in one event.
- Automatically opening a Workspace without confirmation when a deep link targets a different Workspace.
- Copying deep links from Issue-not-found states.
- Issue mutation or changes to Beadwork’s Issue storage model.
- Broad Windows-specific engineering, installer troubleshooting, or acceptance infrastructure if official shared configuration does not provide support trivially.
- Full scroll-state or browser-tab emulation beyond the agreed session Back/Forward model.

## Further Notes

- Triage: `ready-for-agent`.
- The canonical URI intentionally exposes the absolute Workspace path. This is accepted because the link must be self-contained under the current Workspace identity model, but users should understand that copied links can reveal local directory names.
- The design follows the accepted decisions to identify Issues by Workspace path and use Wouter for renderer navigation.
- The existing code currently stores Issue selection and search locally inside Issue Explorer and clears selection whenever search hides a row. Implementation must deliberately move route-owned navigation state to the coordinator and preserve selection for search changes without regressing the distinct view-change behavior.
- The existing Workspace backend already validates candidate paths, resolves Git roots, persists catalog entries, and atomically publishes Current Workspace with Issue Explorer data. Deep-link support should compose that transaction rather than introduce a separate add-or-open path.
- Delivery should be decomposed into independently landable children under this epic before implementation, with dependency ordering that establishes URI/navigation contracts before UI and desktop acceptance work.

comments.text:
## Implementation dependency graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    W1["bsm-wz5.1 Route-owned Issue Explorer"] --> W2["bsm-wz5.2 Back / Forward"]
    W1 --> W3["bsm-wz5.3 Settings route"]
    W1 --> W4["bsm-wz5.4 Issue References"]
    W1 --> W5["bsm-wz5.5 Copy deep links"]
    W2 --> W6["bsm-wz5.6 Same-Workspace deep links"]
    W3 --> W6
    W5 --> W6
    W6 --> W7["bsm-wz5.7 Cross-Workspace deep links"]
    W2 --> W8["bsm-wz5.8 Cross-Workspace history"]
    W7 --> W8
```

All implementation issues are children of this epic and carry `ready-for-agent` triage notation.

title: Deep link to Issues and navigate browsing history
